topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office seemed to hum with a sudden, aggressive frequency. Sarah stared at the presentation slide, the words about "strategic acquisition" blurring into meaningless shapes. Her fingers, resting on the cool laminate of her desk, began to tremble slightly. She tried to focus on the quarterly projections, but the numbers swam, replaced by a frantic internal monologue. Every casual conversation around her—the laughter from the marketing team, the hurried footsteps of the executives—sounded amplified, each word a potential clue. A coldness began to creep up her spine, a physical tightening that made her want to pull her cardigan tighter, even though the air conditioning was working perfectly. She looked up, meeting Mark’s gaze across the room, and for a fleeting second, she saw not a colleague, but a stranger wearing a mask of practiced indifference.

[story 2]

Liam kept re-reading the internal memos, the ink seeming to vibrate under his gaze. He traced the lines of the corporate restructuring plan with his fingertip, his breath catching in his throat with each page turned. The silence of his cubicle felt cavernous, swallowing the usual background noise of the office. He pushed back from his chair abruptly, the scrape of the metal legs against the carpet sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet space. His hands clenched into tight fists at his sides, the knuckles turning white. He walked to the window, looking out at the cityscape, but the view offered no solace; the glass reflected back a distorted image of his own unsettled features. A wave of nausea washed over him, and he had to press the heels of his hands against his eyes, trying to force the visual chaos back into manageable order.

[story 3]

When David finally cornered his supervisor, the air in the small conference room felt thick and unbreathable. David’s voice came out thin, reedy, betraying a tremor he desperately tried to suppress. He kept glancing at the mahogany table, unable to settle his eyes on any single point. He fiddled incessantly with the edge of his water glass, the ice clinking with an irritating, sharp sound. Every time his boss leaned forward, David felt a pressure building behind his sternum, a suffocating weight that made his chest feel constricted. He swallowed hard, the dryness in his mouth making the action painful. He wanted to articulate the dread churning in his gut, but the words refused to form, stuck behind a sudden, paralyzing blockage.
